stories.title: The Ship That Introduced Millions to Cruising
stories.intended_reader: Those who sailed Majesty during her 28-year career
stories.core_insight: Majesty of the Seas made cruising accessible through affordable short sailings from Florida
stories.markdown:
**I boarded Majesty of the Seas for the first time in 1995 with sixty-three dollars in my checking account and a three-night Bahamas cruise that my mother-in-law had paid for as a guilt offering after ruining our wedding reception.**

That's the honest version. The polished version is that we chose Majesty for an affordable getaway. But the truth is messier, the way truth usually is. My wife and I were twenty-four, barely married, and her mother had made a toast at our reception that mentioned her ex-boyfriend. Twice. The cruise was supposed to patch things over.

Majesty didn't care about our family drama. She was 73,941 gross tons of dependable steel, docked in Miami, ready to take us to Nassau and CocoCay and back in three nights for less than we'd spent on the wedding photographer. She'd launched in April 1992 as the third and final Sovereign-class ship—not the first mega-ship (that honor belonged to her sister [Sovereign](/ships/rcl/sovereign-of-the-seas.html)), not the largest (ships were already growing beyond her). But Majesty became something more important than first or biggest. She became the gateway.

That first evening, my wife and I stood in the five-story atrium and looked up. Glass elevators rose through the space like something from a science fiction movie. The two-deck dining room waited below, set with white tablecloths and more silverware than we owned. A pianist played standards on a baby grand while passengers in resort wear drifted past carrying drinks with umbrellas.

We were kids from central Florida who ate at Applebee's on special occasions. This felt like trespassing.

But Majesty's genius was that she never made you feel that way for long. The crew treated our sixty-three-dollar-checking-account selves with the same warmth they gave the couple in the suite. Our waiter—a man from Jamaica named Carlton who laughed at everything, including his own jokes—made us feel like we belonged in a dining room nicer than any restaurant we'd visited.

Three nights. Nassau, where we bought matching shell necklaces for eight dollars. CocoCay, where we swam in water so clear you could count the sand grains. One sea day, where we discovered that the pool deck of a cruise ship on a sunny afternoon is its own kind of paradise.

When we drove home to our apartment, my wife turned to me and said, "We're doing that again." Not a suggestion. A declaration.

We did it again. Eleven more times on Majesty over the next twenty years. Three-night Bahamas loops, four-night versions when we could manage the extra day off work. Always Majesty, always affordable, always the same reliable experience that had hooked us the first time.

Our kids came along starting in 2001. They grew up on Majesty the way some kids grow up at summer camp—it was their annual ritual, their fixed point. Our son took his first steps on Deck 7. Our daughter had her first slow dance at the teen disco. Majesty held these moments like a grandmother holds photographs—carefully, without making a fuss.

Over twenty-eight years, Majesty introduced more first-time cruisers to the industry than nearly any ship in Royal Caribbean's fleet. She was the entry point, the proof of concept, the three-night trial that turned skeptics into loyalists. Many went on to sail larger, newer ships—[Voyager class](/ships/rcl/voyager-of-the-seas.html), [Oasis class](/ships/rcl/oasis-of-the-seas.html), eventually [Icon](/ships/rcl/icon-of-the-seas.html). But they started with Majesty.

She outlasted both her sisters. [Sovereign](/ships/rcl/sovereign-of-the-seas.html) left in 2008. [Monarch](/ships/rcl/monarch-of-the-seas.html) departed in 2013. Majesty kept sailing until 2020, when a pandemic and time finally caught her.

I got the news on my phone—a cruise forum notification that Majesty had been sold and would be scrapped. I was standing in my kitchen, twenty-five years older than the kid who'd boarded with sixty-three dollars, and I felt something unexpected: grief. Not for steel and engines. For the ship that taught me that ordinary people deserve extraordinary things, even if those things only last three nights.

**The lesson:** Some ships don't need to be the biggest or the first. They just need to open the door wide enough for everyone to walk through.

stories.title: What Passengers Remembered
stories.intended_reader: Those who sailed Majesty and want to honor her legacy
stories.core_insight: Majesty's legacy is measured in first cruises, family memories, and accessibility
stories.markdown:
**I started collecting Majesty stories in 2019, a year before she was sold, because I had a feeling we were running out of time and someone ought to write them down before the ship left and the memories scattered.**

I'm a librarian by training. Collecting stories is what I do. But Majesty stories were different from anything I'd cataloged before, because they all started the same way: "It was my first cruise."

A woman named Rosa from Hialeah told me she'd saved for six months in 1995, working double shifts at a hotel laundry, to afford a three-night sailing. "I'd never seen the ocean from the middle," she said. "Only from the beach. Majesty took me to the middle." She paused. "I cried the first night. Not sad crying. The other kind."

A retired teacher from Georgia named Walter described bringing his grandson on Majesty in 2014. The boy was twelve and had never been on a boat of any kind. Walter watched him stand at the railing as the ship pulled away from Port Canaveral and said his grandson's face "looked like Christmas morning, except it lasted for three days."

A couple from New Jersey—married forty-two years—sailed Majesty every year from 1998 to 2018. Twenty consecutive years. Same ship, same three-night Bahamas loop, same cabin category. "People asked why we never tried a different ship," the wife told me. "Why would we? Majesty was *ours*. The crew knew us. The maitre d' had our table ready. The bartender started our drinks when he saw us walk in." She got quiet. "Twenty years of crew who remembered us. You can't buy that. You can only earn it."

The crew stories struck me hardest. Majesty was known for retention—crew members who stayed for ten, fifteen, even twenty years. A housekeeper named Maria from the Philippines worked on Majesty for fourteen years. Fourteen years of making the same beds, cleaning the same cabins, leaving towel animals on the same pillows. I asked her what kept her there. "The guests," she said simply. "They come back. I see their children grow. I see their hair go gray. They are my other family."

Over three thousand cruises. An estimated two-and-a-half to three million passengers. These are the statistics. But statistics don't capture what Rosa felt when she saw the ocean from the middle for the first time, or what Walter's grandson looked like at the railing, or what it means that Maria folded towels for fourteen years and called the passengers her other family.

Majesty's last years were bittersweet. By 2016, she was the oldest ship in Royal Caribbean's active fleet. Voyager class had redefined mega-ships. Oasis class had changed the entire conversation about what a cruise could be. Majesty kept sailing her three-night loops—shorter cruises, budget pricing, first-time cruisers who didn't know or care that the ship was old. They only knew that the atrium still sparkled, the dining room still impressed, and the crew still treated them like they mattered.

She was sold in 2020. The pandemic hit before she could sail again. She was scrapped in Alang, India, in 2021—broken down and recycled, the way ships die when no one saves them.

But the stories survive. Rosa still talks about seeing the ocean from the middle. Walter's grandson is in college now, majoring in marine biology, which Walter attributes entirely to that first Majesty cruise. The couple from New Jersey keeps a photograph of Majesty on their mantelpiece, next to their wedding photo.

I keep all their stories in a box in my office, handwritten and typed, formal and scattered, some tearful and some matter-of-fact. They're the legacy that steel couldn't carry and the shipbreakers couldn't touch.

**The lesson:** A ship's true tonnage isn't measured in gross tons. It's measured in the weight of the memories she carried home.

stories.title: CocoCay Connection
stories.intended_reader: Families who sailed Majesty to Royal Caribbean's private island
stories.core_insight: Majesty of the Seas helped establish CocoCay as a signature Royal Caribbean destination
stories.markdown:
**The photograph sits on my desk: three sunburned kids in matching swimsuits, standing on a Bahamian beach with a cruise ship anchored behind them, and if you look closely you can see that the youngest—my daughter Emma—has sand in her hair and chocolate ice cream on her chin and the most uncomplicated smile I've ever seen on a human face.**

That was CocoCay, 2003. We were on Majesty of the Seas, our second family cruise and our first visit to Royal Caribbean's private island. The kids were nine, seven, and four. My husband and I were the ages we thought were old and now recognize as impossibly young.

Majesty sailed to CocoCay like clockwork. Three-night Bahamas loop: Miami departure, Nassau, CocoCay, return. She'd been running variations of this route since 1992, and by the time we boarded, the crew could have navigated it blindfolded. There was something comforting in that reliability—the knowledge that thousands of families before us had taken this exact voyage and come back happy.

CocoCay in 2003 was simple. No waterparks, no thrill rides, no massive infrastructure. Just beaches, pavilions, a barbecue lunch setup, water sports, and the kind of unstructured freedom that children remember decades after they forget the details.

Emma remembers the sand. She says she remembers how it felt between her toes—soft and warm, different from the gritty sand at Cocoa Beach where we lived. Her older brother Jake remembers the snorkeling, which he insists was the day he decided he wanted to be a diver. Her sister Grace remembers the hammock, because she fell out of it and my husband caught her mid-fall, and she thought he was a superhero for the rest of the trip.

I remember the silence. That sounds wrong—a beach full of cruise passengers isn't quiet. But the particular silence I mean was the absence of worry. No traffic, no crime concerns, no vendors approaching with aggressive sales pitches, no navigating an unfamiliar city with three small children and a stroller. CocoCay offered something cruise ports couldn't: guaranteed safety. A controlled environment where I could let my children run without the low-grade anxiety that accompanies parenting in public spaces.

We sailed Majesty to CocoCay six times between 2001 and 2011. Same ship, same island, same experience—and yet each visit was different because the children were different. In 2001, we were managing nap schedules and diaper bags. By 2011, the kids were teenagers who disappeared into the water sports area and reappeared only when hungry.

The affordability made it possible. Seven-night Caribbean cruises with airfare and port fees would have strained our budget past breaking. Majesty's three-night loops from Port Canaveral meant we could drive to the port, skip the flights, and give the kids a cruise vacation for what we'd spend on a long weekend at Disney. Some years, we sailed twice—spring break and fall break—because the math worked.

That math introduced entire demographics to cruising. Families like ours, working two incomes with three kids and a mortgage that left little room for luxury, discovered through Majesty that luxury didn't require a second mortgage. It required a three-hour drive and a three-night commitment.

CocoCay evolved over the years. By our last visit, Royal Caribbean had added slides, floating platforms, and beach clubs. The simple island we'd first visited was becoming something more elaborate. In 2019, they transformed it into Perfect Day at CocoCay with massive water parks and attractions that would have made my four-year-old Emma lose her mind.

Majesty sailed her last cruises to CocoCay in 2020, right before the pandemic closed everything. Twenty-eight years of delivering families to that beach. Twenty-eight years of sand between toes and chocolate ice cream on chins and photographs that end up on desks where parents sit and remember when their children were small enough to catch mid-fall.

Emma is twenty-six now. She texted me last month: "Mom, do you still have that CocoCay photo? The one where I have ice cream on my face?" I sent it to her. She wrote back: "I want to take my kids there someday."

Majesty is gone. But the beach is still there. And the impulse to bring your children to warm sand and clear water and the particular freedom of a day without worry—that doesn't require a specific ship. It just requires someone who remembers what it felt like to be four years old with ice cream on your chin and the whole ocean ahead of you.

**The lesson:** The best family vacations aren't the ones you plan. They're the ones your children remember when they become parents themselves.

stories.title: The Last Sovereign
stories.intended_reader: Royal Caribbean historians and Sovereign Class enthusiasts
stories.core_insight: Majesty outlasted her sister ships by over a decade, becoming the final Sovereign class vessel in service
stories.markdown:
**Majesty of the Seas holds a unique distinction: she was the last Sovereign-class ship sailing for Royal Caribbean, outlasting her sisters [Sovereign](/ships/rcl/sovereign-of-the-seas.html) and [Monarch](/ships/rcl/monarch-of-the-seas.html) by years.**

**The Sovereign class timeline:**

- **Sovereign of the Seas (1988-2008):** Sold to Pullmantur after 20 years, scrapped 2020
- **Monarch of the Seas (1991-2013):** Sold to Pullmantur after 22 years, scrapped 2020
- **Majesty of the Seas (1992-2020):** Sailed 28 years for Royal Caribbean, sold 2020, scrapped 2021

Majesty outlasted both sisters by 7-12 years—a remarkable achievement for a ship class that launched in the late 1980s.

**Why Majesty lasted:**

1. **Profitability:** Short Bahamas cruises from Florida filled a market niche perfectly. Majesty was reliably profitable year after year.
2. **Efficiency:** Smaller than later mega-ships but perfectly sized for three- and four-night itineraries. No wasted capacity.
3. **Port flexibility:** 880 feet long, 73,941 GT—small enough to fit ports that couldn't handle [Oasis](/ships/rcl/oasis-of-the-seas.html) or [Voyager](/ships/rcl/voyager-of-the-seas.html) class ships.
4. **Crew loyalty:** Majesty retained crew for years, reducing training costs and maintaining consistent service quality.

**The end:**

By 2020, Majesty was 28 years old—ancient by cruise ship standards. Royal Caribbean had [Icon class](/ships/rcl/icon-of-the-seas.html) ships on order. The fleet was evolving toward larger, more innovative vessels.

Majesty was sold to a Greek operator in early 2020. The COVID-19 pandemic hit before she could resume service. She was laid up in Greece, eventually sold for scrap, and broken down in Alang, India in 2021.

**Legacy of the Sovereign class:**

The Sovereign class proved mega-ships could work. They demonstrated that larger vessels with more amenities and higher passenger capacity could still feel intimate and deliver exceptional service.

Majesty, as the last survivor, carried that legacy longer than any other. For 28 years, she proved you didn't need 200,000+ GT to deliver memorable cruises. Sometimes, classic charm, reliable itineraries, and affordable pricing were enough.
